details.text: Derivative: The “وَاو” (وَ) in this verse in the statement وَكِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ (And a clear or clarifying Book) is وَاوُ البَيَّنِيَة (The Waw to clarify) like in English we say “i.e.” or “that is to say” or “in other words” etc.

- The comparison between القُرْآن and الكِتاب. القُرْآن (Al-Qur’an) necessarily implies something that is recited aloud, read or pronounced out loud. قِراءَة (reading) can be undertaken without a paper in front of you which technically differs from تِلاوَة (recitation) as that implies following along which requires a paper to follow, although the terms have become interchangeable.
- القُرْآن emphasises the idea that the Qur’an is something that is heard and not just something that is read.
- الكِتاب necessarily refers to something that is documented and written.
- the use of Both together in This verse provides the explanation for where the verses which are being recited (آيَاتُ الْقُرْءَانِ) come from i.e., from a clear Book (وَكِتَابٍ مُّبِينٍ) which a document which is being sourced in Al-Lawh Al-Mahfoozh (اللَّوْحُ المَّحْفُوظ) – the Preserved Tablet.
Derivative: جَانٌّ can mean: “to come to life” and also “to be a Jinn (in the form of a snake)”. جَانٌّ can also refer to a snake that is of small size but moves quickly.
Derivative: جَيْب means the chest or chest cavity. It is also used for pocket because it was an opening in the garment in the chest area.
Derivative: مُبْصِرَةً means clear to see and illuminated.
Derivative: جَحَدُوا to deny. جَحَدَ means to argue and insist against something despite knowing you are wrong.

- Here كَانَ which is مُذَكَّر (masculine) whilst عَاقِبَة (consequence, outcome or end) is مُؤَنَّث (feminine). That is because when عَاقِبَة is used here it is being used in reference to عَذاب (punishment) which is مُذَكَّر (masculine).

Derivative: يُوزَعُونَ from أَوْزَعَ means to be divided up into divisions and to be ordered and led i.e., in a procession. It also implies stopping and holding back so that everyone knows their place.
Derivative: يَحْطِمُ means to smash and crush. حُطام is what is left over after being crushed.
Derivative: نَمْلَةٌ can refer to the Queen ant that communicates to the other ants.

Derivative: أَوْزِعْنِي from أَوْزَعَ which is a word in the Arabic language that has very diverse meanings. It can imply stopping and holding back so that everyone knows their place just as it can mean the opposite to march and go forth. In a figurative sense إيزاعًا from أَوْزَعَ is also used to mean to inspire somebody to do something good and place something in their heart, which is its meaning here: أَوْزِعْنِي “Inspire in me” i.e., to a good conduct.

- صَالِحًا is a حال (describes the condition accompanying an act) as such the statement أَعْمَلَ صَٰلِحًا “I act Saalih” means not only undertaking a righteous deed but it also refers to the manner of how the deed was undertaken i.e., in a state of righteousness.
Derivative: تَفَقَّدَ means to search or look for something (that is lost) and also to inspect the condition or status of something.
Derivative: الخَبْءَ is that which is hidden in terms of the rain that comes from the sky and the vegetation that comes from the land or ground.

- “And He knows what you hide and what you declare openly”. The reason for mentioning both can be to highlight the inconsistency between what’s really inside the people and what they openly show, where what they say is not a true representation of what they hold inside themselves.

Derivative: يُوقِنُونَ means to have يَقِين (certainty). يَقِين (Yaqeen) encompasses العِلْم (knowledge), the removal of doubt (إِزالةُ الشَّكّ) and a confirmation or verification of the matter (تَحْقِيقُ الأَمْر).

Derivative: تَعْلُوا from عَلا – يَعْلُو – عُلُوٌ in origin means to elevate, raise and be high. In this context it can mean to make advances against someone or something from the meaning of elevating and raising yourself or your status or authority over something and it can also mean to be arrogant with others from the meaning of viewing yourself to be higher and more elevated than others.

Derivative: أَفْتُونِى from أَفْتَى which means to give or pass a فَتْوَى (Fatwa – Verdict). Fatwa (فَتْوَى) carries the meaning of empowering somebody or the recipient of the Fatwa with the ability to make decisions and with the sufficient and clear evidence so that they are capable of making the right decision.

Derivative: مُرْسِلَةٌ is the use of the noun (اِسْم الفاعِل) instead of the verb أُرْسِلُ to mean “I will send” and that indicates here to it being a firm decision that she (i.e., the queen of Sheba) as the noun is stronger than the verb when used for the action.

-لا قِبَلَ is an expression implying the lack of power or ability to face or resist something or someone. لَّا قِبَلَ لَهُمmeans that they possess no power or ability to confront or resist. In this verse it means that we (i.e., Sulaiman) will come to them with an army the like of which they have never faced before and have no power or ability to resist.

Derivative: عِفْرِيتٌ comes from تَعْفِير and the ت (Taa’) at the end is extra and not from the root letters. تَعْفِير التُّراب means to raise a cloud of dirt or dust. In horse races the winning horse would leave those behind in the dust. The winner leaves dust in the face of the one behind. They call him ‘Ifreet (عِفْرِيتٌ) because not only did he win but he also insulted his opponent on the way by raising dust in his face. ‘Ifreet (عِفْرِيتٌ) is also used to describe someone who puts dust on the one who oppose him i.e., humiliates him and makes him eat dust. Here, in this context an ‘Ifreet (عِفْرِيتٌ) from the Jinn means one of the big and strong Jinns.
- it Literally means: “Before you stand up from your place” but its meaning in This context is before the court session comes to an end.
Derivative: يَعْمَهُونَ from عَمِهَ means to wander blindly. عَمِيَ means to be blind of the eyes and عَمِهَ means to be blind of the heart and to become oblivious.
Derivative: نَكِّرُوا from نَكَّرَ means to make something unfamiliar, by changing something to make it look a little different or to disguise.

Derivative: تَهْتَدِي from اِهْتَدَى which means to be guided to something. In this verse it means whether the Queen of Sheba will be guided towards what is correct and recognise her throne as a test of her intellect.
Derivative: لُجَّةٌ refers to the deepest part of the sea or ocean.

Derivative: الصَّرْحَ from صَرَحَ which means to be free of impurities. صَرْح is used to refer to high distinguished or high buildings that are decorated. A lofty imposing structure or palace. It can also refer to a surface here which is made from transparent glass with water beneath it.

Derivative: مُّمَرَّدٌ which means to be made so smooth and clean. Comes from مَرِدَ which means to become smooth like when a young man shaves his beard leaving it smooth or when a branch has all its leaves and extra parts stripped from it leaving it bare and smooth.
Derivative: قَوَارِيرَ refers to glass and crystal.

Derivative: ٱطَّيَّرْنَا originally from تَطَيَّرَ which literally means to attribute a bird which is used to mean: To consider something to be cursed or an evil omen. This is based on the Arabs using birds as signs for good and bad. From this practise the bird came to be seen as bad luck and an evil omen. ٱطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ therefore means: “We have been cursed by you”.
Derivative: تُفْتَنُونَ to mean tested, put to trial and can also mean to be enamoured. مَفْتُون is someone who has been enamoured.

Derivative: رَهْطٍ is a small group composing of between 3-9 individuals. Some have said the number can reach 40. Here it can refer to nine groups which could be families, clans or factions who have influence or power. رَهْطٍ is also used to refer to the leader of the group whilst رَهَطٌ with a Fat’hah upon the Haa’ (ه) refers to the group. As such the verse can be referring to nine men who were the leaders of groups.

Derivative: تَقَاسَمُوا from تَقاسَمَ. قَاسَمَ means to swear (an oath) to someone, like when a person swears that he is speaking the truth. It contains an element of friction where the person is not believed and must keep swearing oaths to be believed. The form تَقاسَمَ indicates cooperation i.e., let’s swear together in cooperation or make an oath together, that we will do such and such a thing or execute such and such a plan etc.
Derivative: الأَخْسَرُونُ is اِسْمُ التَّفْضِيل (superlative noun form). It means the worst losers, those who lose the most or the worst in terms of loss.

Derivative: يَتَطَهَّرُونَ carries the meaning of being self-righteous. The verb is being used like an adjective which implies: “They are trying to be so pure” or “They believe themselves to be so pure”. It is said here in contempt and ridicule.

Derivative: مَّطَرًا means rain however in the Qur’an it is never used in the good and beneficial sense but rather it is used in relation to punishment. Rain in the good sense is described as water (ماءٌ) being sent down from the sky.

Derivative: تُلَقَّى implies the meaning of coming in contact with a teacher and spending time with him until the knowledge of a book is transferred to the student. That is why التَّلَقِّي is used to imply putting something into someone’s heart that takes time. It refers to continuous contact, exposure and receival or absorption.
Derivative: بَهْجَةٍ refers to that which has beauty and brings joy and delight to the one who sees or experiences it.
Derivative: رَوَٰسِىَ means deeply pegged or anchored steady and firm mountains.
Derivative: المُضْطَرَّ is the one who is stuck in a huge amount of difficulty and desperate situation.
Derivative: يُجِيبُ means to respond and implies immediacy.
Derivative: هَاتُوا means to bring and is stronger than other words to bring implying urgency.

Derivative: ادَّارَكَ can mean that their knowledge has reached the depths concerning the hereafter i.e., knowledge of it has come to them bit by bit. It can also be understood to imply that their knowledge will catch up to them and reach them in the hereafter, where ادَّارَكَ means أَدْرَكَ i.e., to the truths that they doubted and blind from in the life of this world. The first understanding affirms their knowledge of the hereafter whilst the second negates it.
Derivative: آنَسْتُ from آنَسَ and it means to figure something out by clues.
Derivative: رَدِفٌ (Radif) comes from رَدِيفٌ (Radeef) who refers to the one who rides behind you. رَدِفٌ (Radif) means it right behind you and is already on its way.
Derivative: تُكِنُّ from أَكَنَّ to hide deep inside or to keep secret. أَكِنَّة are vaults that are impossible to penetrate.
Derivative: حُكْمٌ (Hukm) can mean wisdom, ruling or judgement and authority.
Derivative: تُسْمِعُ from أَسْمَعَ which means to make someone hear or listen.

Derivative: وَقَعَ means to fall or drop and has only come once in a relation to reward within the Qur’an (An-Nisa’: 100), whilst the other times it has come related to punishment and judgements being passed against them.
Derivative: فَوْجًا is a great number or huge legion of people.
Derivative: To be organised, ordered and broken up into different divisions.
Derivative: يَنطِقُونَ from نَطَقَ – يَنْطِقُ – نُطْق which means to speak, to articulate, pronounce or utter a syllable.
Derivative: فَزِعَ means to be terrified, an uncontrollable fear and to be overwhelmed by fear to the extent that you can't think.
Derivative: دَاخِرينَ can encompass the meanings of being in a state of powerless, being humbled, humiliated and can also imply a state of contemptibility and despicability or lowness.
Derivative: أَتْقَنَ means to make, undertake or complete something to perfection and the highest level of proficiency.
Derivative: فَزَعٍ – A great mind-blowing terror.
Derivative: كُبَّتْ to be dropped face first or upside down.
